title: Biosecurity Principles for Smallholders
subtitle: Quarantine, disinfection, and what to do if you suspect a notifiable disease
excerpt: Biosecurity means taking steps to prevent disease from entering your holding and spreading between animals. Good habits protect your stock, your neighbours' stock, and the national herd. This tutorial covers the core principles every keeper should practise.
sourceNotes: APHA biosecurity guidance for livestock keepers; DEFRA Code of Practice for the Prevention and Control of Salmonella; APHA notifiable disease guidance. Synthesised from official UK government publications.

glossaryTerms.definition: A set of practical measures taken to prevent the introduction and spread of disease on a livestock holding.
A period of isolation for newly arrived animals, during which they are kept separate from the main herd or flock to monitor for signs of illness.
A disease that must by law be reported to the Animal and Plant Health Agency (APHA) if you suspect it. Foot-and-mouth, bluetongue, and avian influenza are examples.
A tray or shallow container filled with approved disinfectant solution, placed at the entrance to a livestock area so that footwear is decontaminated on entry and exit.

content.text: What biosecurity means in practice
Good
biosecurity

is mostly habit. It is not expensive. The goal is to reduce the number of routes by which pathogens can reach your animals. Most disease arrives through new animals, visiting people, and shared equipment.
Quarantine for new arrivals
Every animal new to your holding should go through a
quarantine period
before joining the main group. A minimum of 28 days is the standard recommendation. During quarantine, the animal is housed separately, with no nose-to-nose contact with your existing stock.
Handle quarantined animals last. Use separate tools, buckets, and clothing for the quarantine pen. Wash your hands and change footwear before returning to the main herd.
During quarantine, monitor for coughing, nasal discharge, diarrhoea, lameness, or skin lesions. Call your vet if any appear. Starting treatment early often prevents spread.
Visitor footwear and access control

Anyone entering your livestock area can bring pathogens on their boots. This includes vets, feed deliveries, shearers, and friends. Provide clean wellies for visitors to change into. Keep a note of who visits, when, and which animals they had contact with. If a disease outbreak is traced back to your area, this log will help APHA enormously.
Place a
disinfectant mat
at each entrance to your livestock area. Change the solution at least weekly. A weak, old solution gives a false sense of security. Follow the manufacturer's dilution instructions.
Cleaning vehicles and equipment

Trailers and livestock vehicles carry pathogens from the last place they were used. Clean and disinfect your trailer after every trip. This means washing off all organic matter first. Disinfectant does not work through mud and dung. Use a pressure washer, then apply an approved disinfectant, then allow to dry.
Do not lend your trailer without cleaning it first on return. Do not borrow someone else's trailer without cleaning it before use.
Any shared equipment including hurdles, feeders, and water troughs should be cleaned before it arrives on your holding. Ask where it was last used before accepting it.
Rodent and pest control

Rodents carry Salmonella, Leptospirosis, and other diseases. They contaminate feed and water. Store all feed in sealed metal bins. Clear feed spillages promptly. Check for rodent entry points in feed stores and block them. A professional pest contractor is worth the cost if you have a persistent problem.
Recognising a notifiable disease
Some diseases must by law be reported to APHA immediately if suspected. These are
notifiable disease
s. You do not need to be certain. Suspicion is enough to trigger reporting duty. Common examples include:
Foot-and-mouth disease: blisters on the mouth, feet, and teats; sudden lameness in several animals
Bluetongue: facial swelling, ulcers, reddening of skin, lameness in sheep and cattle
Avian influenza: sudden deaths in poultry, severe breathing problems, falling egg numbers
Swine vesicular disease: similar to foot-and-mouth but in pigs only

If you suspect any notifiable disease, stop all movements on and off your holding immediately. Do not wait for test results. Call the APHA helpline on 03000 200 301. Moving animals when a notifiable disease is suspected is a criminal offence.
A simple daily biosecurity checklist
Check disinfectant mat on entry to livestock area
Use dedicated footwear for animal areas
Observe all animals for any signs of illness
Remove feed spillages before leaving the area
Record any visitor who enters the livestock area
